heroOverview: Hepatoblastoma is the most common primary liver cancer in children under five years of age, arising from embryonic liver precursor cells and accounting for roughly 79% of all paediatric liver malignancies. The global incidence is approximately 1–2 cases per million children per year, with an unexplained gradual increase of about 2–3% annually over recent decades that is most pronounced in very-low-birth-weight infants. Most children present before their third birthday with an asymptomatic abdominal mass discovered by a parent or during a routine examination. With modern combined-modality therapy — cisplatin-based chemotherapy followed by surgical resection — the overall five-year survival rate for localised disease now exceeds 90%, making early detection and prompt specialist referral critical determinants of outcome.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1–2 cases per million children under 15 per year; roughly 100–150 new cases diagnosed annually in the United States (SEER data, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects children under 5 years; median age at diagnosis is 16–18 months; slight male predominance (male-to-female ratio approximately 1.7:1)
keyStats.avgAge: Median diagnosis age 16–18 months; more than 90% of cases occur before age 5
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1,000–1,500 new cases per year worldwide; incidence rising approximately 2–3% per year, especially in preterm infants

definition: Hepatoblastoma (ICD-10: C22.2) is a malignant embryonal tumour of the liver arising from pluripotent hepatic precursor cells during early hepatogenesis. It falls under paediatric oncology and is the most frequent primary hepatic malignancy of childhood. Histologically, hepatoblastoma is classified primarily as epithelial (comprising fetal, embryonal, macrotrabecular, and small-cell undifferentiated subtypes) or mixed epithelial–mesenchymal (which may contain osteoid, cartilage, or teratoid elements); small-cell undifferentiated histology confers the worst prognosis. At the molecular level, activating mutations in the Wnt–beta-catenin signalling pathway are present in approximately 70–90% of tumours. Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), a serum glycoprotein normally produced by fetal hepatocytes, is markedly elevated in more than 90% of hepatoblastomas and serves both as a diagnostic marker and a highly reliable treatment response and surveillance marker: AFP should fall by at least 1 log unit per chemotherapy cycle and normalise after complete resection. The tumour is staged using the PRETEXT (PRE-Treatment EXTent of disease) system, which defines resectability based on the number of liver sections involved and the presence of vascular, extrahepatic, or metastatic extension. Approximately 20% of patients have metastases at diagnosis, most commonly to the lungs. Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, hemihypertrophy, familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), and very-low-birth-weight (VLBW, <1,500 g) prematurity are the strongest known risk factors. Treatment is coordinated by a multidisciplinary team including paediatric oncology, paediatric surgery or liver transplantation, diagnostic radiology, and pathology.

typesClassification.description: Composed of cells closely resembling normal fetal hepatocytes arranged in thin trabeculae; associated with the best prognosis of all subtypes and may be curable by resection alone in low-stage disease.
More primitive cells than fetal subtype with higher mitotic activity; the most common epithelial subtype overall; responds well to chemotherapy.

Contains both hepatocellular components and mesenchymal elements (bone, cartilage, or fibrous tissue); overall prognosis is intermediate; the presence of teratoid features does not worsen outcome significantly.
The least differentiated and most aggressive subtype; frequently AFP-negative; associated with younger age at diagnosis and the poorest prognosis; requires intensified treatment regimens.
Characterised by thick trabeculae of tumour cells; can overlap with hepatocellular carcinoma morphologically; intermediate prognosis depending on stage.

primarySymptoms: An abdominal mass or distension — the most common presenting finding, typically noticed by a parent or detected at a routine well-child examination; the mass is usually firm, smooth, non-tender, and confined to the right upper quadrant or crosses the midline in large tumours.
Abdominal pain or discomfort in a subset of children, occurring when the tumour is large enough to compress adjacent structures or when subcapsular haemorrhage occurs.
Weight loss or poor weight gain (failure to thrive) reflecting the metabolic burden of a rapidly growing malignancy on a young child's nutritional reserves.
Loss of appetite (anorexia), often noticed by parents as reduced interest in feeding or unusual fussiness at mealtimes.
Nausea and vomiting caused by compression of the duodenum or stomach by a large hepatic mass, occasionally mimicking gastrointestinal obstruction.
Visible or palpable abdominal fullness with progressive abdominal enlargement, sometimes accompanied by stretched, shiny overlying skin.
Pallor and fatigue reflecting anaemia — common in advanced hepatoblastoma — caused by tumour-related suppression of erythropoiesis or by chronic illness.

Jaundice (yellowing of the skin and sclera), which is relatively uncommon in hepatoblastoma but occurs when large tumours obstruct the biliary tree or when there is extensive hepatic parenchymal replacement.

Precocious puberty in boys, an uncommon but distinctive paraneoplastic feature caused by beta-human chorionic gonadotrophin (beta-hCG) secretion by the tumour, producing testicular enlargement and early secondary sexual characteristics.
Fever without an identified infectious source, particularly with large or necrotic tumours, reflecting systemic inflammatory response to the malignancy.
earlyWarningSigns: An unexplained firm swelling in the right side of the abdomen of an infant or toddler, even in the absence of pain.
Persistent abdominal distension not explained by constipation, gas, or feeding changes.
Unexplained weight faltering or feeding difficulty in a child under three years.
A new elevated serum AFP level on routine screening in a child with known Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome or hemihypertrophy.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe abdominal pain with peritoneal signs (rigidity, guarding, rebound tenderness) indicating tumour rupture and haemoperitoneum — a surgical emergency.
Rapidly progressive respiratory distress in a child with known hepatoblastoma, which may indicate large pulmonary metastases or massive pleural effusion.
Signs of severe anaemia or haemodynamic instability — extreme pallor, tachycardia, hypotension — requiring immediate blood transfusion and urgent oncologic assessment.

causes.cause: Activation of the Wnt–beta-catenin signalling pathway
causes.description: Gain-of-function mutations in the CTNNB1 gene encoding beta-catenin are found in 70–90% of hepatoblastomas; uncontrolled beta-catenin activity promotes embryonal hepatocyte proliferation and prevents normal apoptosis, providing the central oncogenic driver of tumour formation.

Children born weighing less than 1,500 g have a 20- to 40-fold increased risk of hepatoblastoma compared to term infants; the mechanism is believed to involve abnormal hepatic development related to prematurity, hyperoxia from supplemental oxygen, and disrupted normal hepatocyte differentiation in an immature liver.

This overgrowth syndrome caused by dysregulation of chromosome 11p15 genomic imprinting is associated with a 2,000- to 2,500-fold increase in hepatoblastoma risk; it is estimated that 5–10% of all hepatoblastoma cases occur in children with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, and surveillance AFP and ultrasound are therefore performed routinely in these children.

causes.cause: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) and APC mutations
causes.description: Germline mutations in the APC gene — which negatively regulates beta-catenin — predispose to hepatoblastoma at a 750- to 7,500-fold elevated relative risk; hepatoblastoma may be the presenting manifestation of FAP in a young child before colonic polyps develop.

Isolated hemihypertrophy (one side of the body growing disproportionately) is associated with an approximately 3–5% lifetime risk of hepatoblastoma and warrants surveillance screening; the molecular basis overlaps with Beckwith-Wiedemann spectrum disorders.

riskFactors.description: The strongest postnatal risk factor; relative risk of hepatoblastoma is 20–40 times that of normal-birth-weight infants; the risk appears inversely correlated with birth weight within the VLBW range.

An imprinting disorder at chromosome 11p15 characterised by macrosomia, macroglossia, omphalocele, and predisposition to embryonal tumours; hepatoblastoma occurs in approximately 1–3% of BWS patients and surveillance is mandatory.

Germline APC loss-of-function activates the same Wnt–beta-catenin pathway disrupted somatically in sporadic hepatoblastoma; children of FAP-affected parents should have AFP and liver ultrasound surveillance.
Isolated lateralised overgrowth without other BWS features confers a several-fold elevated hepatoblastoma risk; these children are enrolled in embryonal tumour surveillance programmes.
Boys are affected approximately 1.7 times as often as girls, for reasons that are not fully understood but may relate to sex-linked differences in liver development and Wnt signalling.
Trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome), trisomy 21, and other chromosomal aneuploidy syndromes are over-represented in hepatoblastoma series, though the absolute numbers are small.

affectedDemographics: Infants and toddlers under 3 years of age, who account for the majority of diagnoses
Children with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome on AFP and ultrasound surveillance
Children with familial adenomatous polyposis in their family history
Boys, who are diagnosed approximately 1.7 times more often than girls
Children with isolated hemihypertrophy undergoing embryonal tumour surveillance
diagnosisOverview: The diagnostic evaluation of a suspected hepatoblastoma is a coordinated process that begins with measurement of serum AFP and cross-sectional liver imaging and concludes with tumour biopsy for histological and molecular confirmation. AFP is the critical first-line biomarker: it is elevated above age-adjusted normal values in more than 90% of hepatoblastomas, often dramatically so (values of 100,000–1,000,000 ng/mL are common). Interpreting AFP requires age adjustment because AFP is normally very high in neonates and declines to adult reference ranges by approximately 6–12 months. Ultrasound is the initial imaging modality; it rapidly identifies a hepatic mass, assesses its echogenicity, and evaluates major vessel involvement. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and chest and MRI of the liver are used for formal PRETEXT staging, with MRI now preferred for detailed liver segmental anatomy. CT of the chest is mandatory to evaluate for pulmonary metastases, which are present in approximately 20% of children at diagnosis and do not preclude cure. Bone scintigraphy or whole-body MRI is performed when extrahepatic disease is extensive. Biopsy — typically a percutaneous core needle biopsy or, when resection is immediately feasible, an intraoperative specimen — is essential for histological subtyping and molecular analysis, and distinguishes hepatoblastoma from other hepatic masses in childhood (hepatocellular carcinoma, mesenchymal hamartoma, focal nodular hyperplasia, hepatic haemangioma). PRETEXT staging defines the number of contiguous liver sections (I–IV) free of tumour and documents the presence of vascular involvement (annotation factors V and P), extrahepatic abdominal disease (E), multifocality (F), lymph node involvement (N), and metastases (M), guiding resectability assessment and chemotherapy intensity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary tumour marker; elevated in over 90% of hepatoblastomas, often markedly so; used for diagnosis, chemotherapy response monitoring (should fall at least 1 log per cycle), post-resection surveillance for relapse, and cure confirmation (normalisation).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw from the child's arm or hand vein; results available within hours. Age-adjusted interpretation is essential — neonatal AFP can normally exceed 100,000 ng/mL and must be compared against age-specific reference ranges.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging to confirm a hepatic mass, assess echogenicity and vascularity with Doppler, and evaluate major vessel involvement as a rapid, radiation-free initial step.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel is applied to the child's abdomen and a transducer is moved across the skin painlessly for 15–30 minutes; sedation is not usually required in cooperative children.

diagnosticTests.test: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and chest
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides formal PRETEXT staging by defining tumour extent across liver segments, documents vascular invasion, and screens for pulmonary metastases (the most common site of distant spread).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The child lies still in the scanner for a few minutes; an iodinated contrast agent is injected intravenously; sedation or general anaesthesia may be needed in young children.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The preferred modality for detailed liver segmental anatomy, vascular relationship mapping, and PRETEXT annotation; superior soft-tissue resolution aids surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A longer examination than CT, typically 30–60 minutes; general anaesthesia is usually required for children under 6 due to the need for breath-hold sequences.

diagnosticTests.test: Core needle biopsy of the tumour
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides tissue for histological subtyping (fetal, embryonal, SCUD, mixed) and molecular analysis essential for risk stratification and treatment planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under image guidance (ultrasound or CT) with the child under general anaesthesia; a thin needle is passed through the abdominal wall into the tumour to obtain small tissue cylinders; the child is monitored for 4–6 hours afterwards.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), beta-hCG, liver function tests
diagnosticTests.purpose: LDH correlates with tumour burden; beta-hCG is elevated in the rare precocious puberty variant; liver function tests assess residual hepatic function ahead of surgical or transplantation planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draws; results available within hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Germline testing for APC, CTNNB1, and 11p15 methylation (in selected cases)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies familial risk syndromes (FAP, BWS) that have implications for family counselling and surveillance of siblings.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood or saliva sample sent to a genetics laboratory; results may take several weeks.

treatmentOverview: Hepatoblastoma is treated through a tightly coordinated multidisciplinary protocol in which systemic chemotherapy and surgical resection are the two central pillars. The standard international approach, as defined by the International Childhood Liver Tumours Strategy Group (SIOPEL), the Children's Oncology Group (COG), and the Japanese Study Group for Pediatric Liver Tumors (JPLT), is to administer neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy before surgery in all but the smallest, most clearly resectable tumours (PRETEXT I and selected PRETEXT II with purely fetal histology, who may proceed to immediate surgery). The goal of neoadjuvant chemotherapy is to reduce tumour size and vascularity, lower AFP to facilitate response monitoring, eradicate micrometastatic disease, and convert an initially unresectable tumour to a resectable one. The most widely used regimen is PLADO (cisplatin plus doxorubicin) or cisplatin monotherapy for standard-risk disease, and SIOPEL-6 or COG high-risk protocols (which may add carboplatin, etoposide, or vincristine) for advanced disease. AFP response is assessed after each cycle; failure to decline appropriately by at least 1 log unit per cycle triggers biopsy reassessment and protocol modification. After 3–4 cycles of chemotherapy, resectability is re-evaluated: complete surgical resection is required for cure, because no systemic therapy alone achieves long-term remission. Resection options include formal hepatic resection (right hepatectomy, trisegmentectomy, or extended resections) and, for tumours involving all four liver sections (PRETEXT IV), liver transplantation — which provides excellent long-term outcomes with five-year survival rates of 65–85% in this setting. Pulmonary metastases that persist after chemotherapy require resection (pulmonary metastasectomy). Ablative techniques (radiofrequency ablation) may be used in selected recurrent cases. The target AFP at end of treatment is below 15 ng/mL; persistent elevation indicates residual or recurrent disease. Radiation therapy has a very limited role and is used only in specific salvage situations.

medicalTreatments.name: Cisplatin-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (PLADO or cisplatin monotherapy)
medicalTreatments.description: The standard first-line systemic treatment given before surgery; cisplatin plus doxorubicin (PLADO) or cisplatin alone (standard-risk SIOPEL protocol) is administered in 3–4 cycles before planned resection; AFP decline per cycle is the key response indicator.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Tumour downstaging achieved in approximately 70–80% of initially unresectable hepatoblastomas in SIOPEL and COG trials; 5-year overall survival above 90% for standard-risk disease.

medicalTreatments.name: High-risk chemotherapy intensification (carboplatin, etoposide, doxorubicin combinations)
medicalTreatments.description: Applied in high-risk hepatoblastoma: metastatic disease, PRETEXT IV, AFP <100 ng/mL at diagnosis (SCUD), or poor AFP response; protocols include SIOPEL-3 HR, COG AHEP0731, or the PHITT trial regimens.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 5-year overall survival approximately 55–70% in high-risk/metastatic hepatoblastoma with aggressive multimodal therapy; ongoing trials seek to improve outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Sorafenib (targeted therapy)
medicalTreatments.description: A multikinase inhibitor used in refractory or relapsed hepatoblastoma as salvage therapy; targets VEGFR and RAF signalling pathways; use is extrapolated from adult hepatocellular carcinoma data and small paediatric case series.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Objective response rates of approximately 20–30% in heavily pre-treated relapsed cases in published case series; not established as standard first-line therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Cisplatin and doxorubicin consolidation chemotherapy (post-resection)
medicalTreatments.description: Additional adjuvant cycles given after complete resection in standard protocols to eliminate residual microscopic disease; number of cycles determined by pre-operative PRETEXT stage and histology.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: When added to complete resection, contributes to overall 5-year survival above 90% in standard-risk disease per SIOPEL-6 and COG AHEP0731 outcomes data.

surgicalOptions.name: Hepatic resection (partial hepatectomy)
surgicalOptions.description: The standard surgical procedure for hepatoblastoma; encompasses right hepatectomy, left hepatectomy, extended hepatectomy, or segmental resections; performed after neoadjuvant chemotherapy in the majority of patients; a clear (R0) resection margin is required for cure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resection with clear margins is achieved in approximately 85–90% of patients who reach surgery after neoadjuvant chemotherapy; 5-year survival after complete resection exceeds 90% for standard-risk disease.

surgicalOptions.name: Liver transplantation (orthotopic)
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for PRETEXT IV tumours where the entire liver is involved and a curative resection leaving adequate liver remnant is not feasible; also used in selected cases of unresectable relapse or in cases where extended resection would leave insufficient functional liver volume.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year overall survival approximately 65–85% for hepatoblastoma treated by transplantation in international registry data (Super-urgency ILTS / SPLIT registry studies); superior to salvage chemotherapy alone in unresectable cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Pulmonary metastasectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical resection of pulmonary metastases that persist after primary chemotherapy; performed after hepatic resection or transplantation when metastases have responded partially but not completely; often involves multiple wedge resections or thoracoscopic procedures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Lung metastases resected after cisplatin chemotherapy achieve complete remission in approximately 75–80% of patients with initial pulmonary disease in COG and SIOPEL data.

alternativeTreatments.name: Radiofrequency ablation (RFA) or microwave ablation
alternativeTreatments.description: Minimally invasive heat-based ablation techniques used for small, localised recurrent tumours not suitable for resection; not a substitute for standard resection but a salvage option in selected recurrent cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Transarterial chemoembolisation (TACE)
alternativeTreatments.description: Intra-arterial delivery of chemotherapy combined with embolic particles to the tumour's hepatic arterial supply; used as a bridging or downstaging strategy in selected cases, more commonly reported in Asian hepatoblastoma protocols.

whySeeSpecialist: Any infant or young child with an unexplained abdominal mass must be referred urgently to a specialist paediatric oncology centre with expertise in hepatoblastoma. Outcomes are strongly linked to the quality and timeliness of PRETEXT staging, the expertise of the paediatric surgical and transplantation team, and access to specialised chemotherapy protocols. Community or district hospitals should not attempt resection without specialist consultation. Children with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome or familial FAP should be enrolled in formal AFP and ultrasound surveillance programmes at a specialist centre from birth or diagnosis.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek care at a specialist paediatric cancer centre that is a member of an international liver tumour study group (SIOPEL, COG, or JPLT) and has a high-volume paediatric liver surgery and transplantation programme. The multidisciplinary team should include a paediatric oncologist experienced in embryonal liver tumours, a paediatric hepatobiliary surgeon or transplant surgeon, a paediatric radiologist with expertise in liver MRI and PRETEXT staging, and a pathologist with paediatric tumour subspecialty. Confirm that the centre participates in current clinical trials (PHITT international trial where available) to maximise access to evolving protocols.
preventionStrategies: Enrol children with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome into AFP and liver ultrasound surveillance every three months from birth until at least age four, as recommended by the BWS Consensus Guidelines.
Screen children with familial adenomatous polyposis or known germline APC mutations with AFP and ultrasound surveillance from birth as per COG FAP screening protocols.
Enrol children with isolated hemihypertrophy into a structured embryonal tumour surveillance programme.
Ensure adequate paediatric follow-up for children born at very low birth weight (<1,500 g), including awareness of hepatoblastoma as a potential delayed complication.

For survivors of hepatoblastoma, adhere to structured long-term follow-up protocols monitoring for relapse (AFP and imaging), chemotherapy-related complications (hearing loss from cisplatin, cardiac toxicity from doxorubicin), and secondary malignancies.
lifestyleModifications: During active chemotherapy, protect against infection: practice careful hand hygiene, avoid crowds and unwell contacts, and seek immediate medical attention for fever above 38°C (febrile neutropenia protocol).
Maintain the best possible nutritional intake during chemotherapy — high-calorie, high-protein foods, and when necessary, enteral supplementation — because malnutrition worsens treatment tolerance.
Engage with a paediatric psychosocial support team from the start of treatment to address the emotional burden on both child and family.
After completion of treatment, maintain adherence to long-term follow-up appointments as cisplatin-related hearing loss requires audiological monitoring and may necessitate hearing aids.
Keep vaccination records current, noting that live vaccines are withheld during immunosuppressive chemotherapy and only resumed after adequate immune reconstitution.

recommended: High-calorie, high-protein diet to support tissue repair and immune function during chemotherapy
Small, frequent meals to manage nausea from cisplatin-based regimens
Nutritional supplements or enteral tube feeding if oral intake is insufficient to maintain growth velocity
Adequate hydration to support renal cisplatin excretion and reduce nephrotoxicity risk
avoid: Unpasteurised dairy products, raw eggs, and undercooked meat during periods of neutropenia
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice, which can affect metabolism of certain chemotherapy agents
Excessive salt intake in children with fluid retention from albumin depletion or steroid use
Herbal supplements or high-dose alternative remedies not approved by the oncology team, as interactions with chemotherapy are possible

exerciseGuidelines: During active intravenous chemotherapy, physical activity should be adapted to the child's energy and tolerance, avoiding contact sports while platelet counts are low (below 50,000/µL). When counts recover, gentle age-appropriate activity — supervised play, walking — is encouraged because physical activity supports nutritional status, mood, and functional recovery. After completion of treatment and surgical recovery, progressive return to normal childhood activity is actively encouraged. Long-term survivors should receive physiotherapy review if post-operative hepatic or thoracic surgery has affected their mobility or respiratory function.
dailyManagement: Attend all scheduled chemotherapy cycles, AFP measurements, and imaging assessments on time; delays can compromise protocol integrity and outcomes.
Monitor for fever above 38°C and report it immediately to the oncology team as a potential febrile neutropenia emergency.
Watch for and report signs of hearing change after cisplatin cycles; audiological testing is performed routinely but parental reporting accelerates detection.
Maintain the AFP monitoring log across treatment cycles to track response; bring the log to each clinic visit.
After surgical resection, follow wound care and activity restriction instructions precisely, particularly regarding abdominal protection during the healing phase.
prognosis: Hepatoblastoma outcomes have improved dramatically over the past three decades and continue to improve through international collaborative trials. For standard-risk localised disease (PRETEXT I–III, non-metastatic, AFP >100 ng/mL), the five-year overall survival rate exceeds 90% with cisplatin-based chemotherapy and complete surgical resection. PRETEXT IV disease and metastatic disease have a five-year survival of approximately 55–70% with high-risk protocols and liver transplantation. Small-cell undifferentiated histology (AFP-low tumours) carries the worst prognosis at approximately 30–45% five-year survival even with intensified treatment. Relapsed hepatoblastoma is challenging to treat, with durable second remission achieved in only 20–30% of cases. Long-term survivorship is complicated by cisplatin-related bilateral sensorineural hearing loss (affecting approximately 30–50% of treated children, particularly those treated before age two), doxorubicin-related subclinical cardiac dysfunction, and the risk of secondary malignancies, which require structured long-term follow-up care.
recoveryTimeline: Most children receiving neoadjuvant chemotherapy complete four cycles before surgery over a period of approximately 16–18 weeks. Hepatic resection carries a recovery period of four to six weeks before adjuvant chemotherapy resumes. Total treatment duration from diagnosis to end of planned chemotherapy is typically six to nine months for standard-risk disease and nine to twelve months for high-risk or transplant patients. After completion of treatment, AFP monitoring and liver imaging continue every three months for the first two years, then every six months through year five, reflecting the period of highest relapse risk. Audiological and cardiac follow-up continues annually for a decade or longer.
complications: Cisplatin-related bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, the most common and impactful treatment toxicity, affecting approximately 30–50% of treated patients and often requiring hearing aids in young children at critical language development ages.

Doxorubicin-related cardiomyopathy — subclinical ventricular dysfunction detectable by echocardiography in a proportion of long-term survivors — requiring lifelong cardiac surveillance and restriction of cumulative anthracycline dose.
Tumour rupture with haemoperitoneum — a rare but life-threatening emergency at presentation, requiring emergency surgical control and intensified post-operative management.

Post-hepatectomy liver insufficiency — the risk of inadequate liver remnant after extensive resection, mitigated by careful volumetric planning and, when needed, portal vein embolisation to induce compensatory hypertrophy of the future liver remnant.
Pulmonary metastases at diagnosis or relapse, which are resectable in many cases but add treatment complexity and adversely affect prognosis.
Tumour relapse — occurring in approximately 15–25% of patients within two years of completing treatment; early detection via AFP rise precedes imaging findings and enables prompt salvage therapy.

supportResources.name: Children's Oncology Group — Hepatoblastoma Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Parent and patient resources from the leading North American paediatric oncology cooperative group, including information on active hepatoblastoma trials and survivorship care plans.

supportResources.name: National Cancer Institute — Childhood Liver Cancer Treatment (PDQ)
supportResources.description: NCI patient-facing overview of childhood liver cancer including hepatoblastoma diagnosis, staging, treatment options, and supportive care.

supportResources.name: Cancer Research UK — Hepatoblastoma in Children
supportResources.description: Plain-English guide to hepatoblastoma symptoms, treatment, and prognosis for parents and carers.

sources.title: Meyers RL et al. Hepatoblastoma state of the art: pre-treatment extent of disease, surgical resection guidelines and the role of liver transplantation. Current Opinion in Pediatrics, 2014
Czauderna P et al. Hepatoblastoma state of the art: pathology, genetics, risk stratification, and chemotherapy. Current Opinion in Pediatrics, 2014
Hiyama E et al. Pediatric hepatic tumors: changing aspects. Cancers, 2017
Zsiros J et al. Dose-dense cisplatin-based chemotherapy and surgery for children with hepatoblastoma (SIOPEL-3): a randomised, controlled, phase 3 trial. Lancet Oncology, 2013
National Cancer Institute. Childhood Liver Cancer Treatment PDQ (Health Professional Version). NCI, 2024
Towbin AJ et al. 2017 PRETEXT: radiologic staging system for primary hepatic malignancies of childhood revised for the Paediatric Hepatic International Tumour Trial (PHITT). Pediatric Radiology, 2018
Tiao GM and Bobey N. Current management of hepatoblastoma. Current Opinion in Oncology, 2005

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hepatitis causes liver inflammation with jaundice, elevated transaminases, and systemic illness, while hepatoblastoma presents as an abdominal mass with markedly elevated AFP and minimal liver enzyme disruption in a child under five.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leukaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leukaemia presents with bone marrow failure, blood count abnormalities, and widespread disease, whereas hepatoblastoma is a localised hepatic mass with dramatically elevated AFP on imaging and normal blood counts until disease is advanced.

confusedWithConditions.name: Brain tumour
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Brain tumours produce neurological symptoms and cranial imaging findings, while hepatoblastoma is a hepatic mass diagnosed by abdominal imaging and AFP, with no neurological involvement.

relatedConditions.name: Leukaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common childhood malignancy; diagnosed in the same paediatric oncology setting as hepatoblastoma and part of the differential in a child presenting with anaemia and systemic symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Lymphoma can cause hepatomegaly and systemic symptoms in children, and is part of the differential diagnosis for abdominal mass in childhood; staging overlaps with the hepatoblastoma workup.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Viral hepatitis is screened for during hepatoblastoma assessment and is relevant in the long-term management of liver-transplanted survivors who require immunosuppression.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic hepatitis B
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic hepatitis B is a known risk factor for adult hepatocellular carcinoma and is assessed in any child presenting with a liver mass to ensure the correct diagnosis of hepatoblastoma versus other hepatic malignancies.

faqs.question: What is hepatoblastoma?
faqs.answer: Hepatoblastoma is the most common liver cancer in young children, usually diagnosed before age 5. It arises from embryonic liver cells and typically presents as a firm abdominal mass. More than 90% of tumours produce a protein called alpha-fetoprotein that is used as a diagnosis and treatment response marker.

faqs.question: What causes hepatoblastoma?
faqs.answer: Most hepatoblastomas arise from mutations that activate the Wnt–beta-catenin growth signalling pathway in developing liver cells. Specific risk factors include very-low-birth-weight prematurity, Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, familial adenomatous polyposis, and hemihypertrophy. In most cases no inherited cause is identified.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of hepatoblastoma?
faqs.answer: The most common sign is a firm, painless abdominal mass noticed by a parent. Other symptoms include abdominal swelling, loss of appetite, weight loss, nausea, and pallor. Jaundice is uncommon. In rare cases, a tumour secreting beta-hCG can cause early puberty in young boys.

faqs.question: How is hepatoblastoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis involves measuring alpha-fetoprotein in blood — which is markedly elevated in more than 90% of cases — combined with abdominal ultrasound, contrast CT or liver MRI for PRETEXT staging, CT of the chest for metastases, and tumour biopsy for histological confirmation.

faqs.question: How is hepatoblastoma treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines cisplatin-based chemotherapy to shrink the tumour before surgery, followed by surgical resection. Liver transplantation is used when the entire liver is involved. Pulmonary metastases that persist after chemotherapy are surgically removed. Treatment is overseen by a paediatric oncology team.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for hepatoblastoma?
faqs.answer: For localised, standard-risk hepatoblastoma, the 5-year survival rate exceeds 90% with current chemotherapy and surgery. Children with spread to the lungs or throughout the liver have a 5-year survival of approximately 55–70% with intensive treatment. These figures are based on international cooperative group trials.

faqs.question: What is AFP and why is it important in hepatoblastoma?
faqs.answer: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is a protein produced by embryonic liver cells that is highly elevated in over 90% of hepatoblastomas. It is used to help diagnose the tumour, monitor how well chemotherapy is working — it should fall by at least 10-fold per cycle — and detect relapse after treatment. Normalisation of AFP after surgery suggests complete removal.

faqs.question: Is hepatoblastoma hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most cases are sporadic with no family history. However, hepatoblastoma risk is significantly elevated in children with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, familial adenomatous polyposis, and isolated hemihypertrophy — all of which can be inherited. Children with these conditions should receive AFP and ultrasound surveillance from birth.

faqs.question: Can hepatoblastoma spread to other parts of the body?
faqs.answer: About 20% of children have metastases at diagnosis, most commonly to the lungs. Bone and brain metastases are rare. Pulmonary metastases that respond to chemotherapy are often surgically removed and do not preclude cure in most cases.

faqs.question: What is PRETEXT staging for hepatoblastoma?
faqs.answer: PRETEXT stands for PRE-Treatment EXTent of disease. It describes how many of the four liver sections are involved by tumour (PRETEXT I–IV, with IV meaning all four sections are involved) and documents vascular invasion, extrahepatic spread, and metastases. PRETEXT determines whether immediate surgery or chemotherapy first is planned.

faqs.question: Why might a child with hepatoblastoma need a liver transplant?
faqs.answer: If the tumour involves all four segments of the liver (PRETEXT IV) and cannot be removed while leaving enough healthy liver behind, transplantation provides the only route to complete tumour removal. Five-year survival after transplantation for hepatoblastoma is approximately 65–85%, making it a well-established curative option.

faqs.question: What are the long-term side effects of hepatoblastoma treatment?
faqs.answer: Cisplatin causes bilateral hearing loss in approximately 30–50% of treated children, often requiring hearing aids. Doxorubicin can affect heart function over time, requiring regular cardiac monitoring. Liver surgery can affect growth and nutrition. All survivors need long-term specialist follow-up for these complications.

faqs.question: What is the role of chemotherapy in hepatoblastoma?
faqs.answer: Chemotherapy — mainly cisplatin — is given before surgery to shrink the tumour and make resection possible. AFP response after each cycle monitors treatment effectiveness. Additional chemotherapy cycles are given after resection to eliminate any remaining microscopic disease. Chemotherapy is essential in all but the smallest low-risk tumours.

faqs.question: How do doctors monitor for hepatoblastoma recurrence?
faqs.answer: After completing treatment, children have blood AFP tests and liver imaging (ultrasound or CT/MRI) every three months for the first two years, then every six months through year five. AFP rise before imaging changes is often the first sign of relapse, allowing early re-intervention.

faqs.question: Can hepatoblastoma come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Relapse occurs in approximately 15–25% of patients within the first two years after completing therapy. Relapsed hepatoblastoma is challenging to treat; second remissions are achieved in about 20–30% of relapsed cases. This is why strict follow-up surveillance for five years is maintained after completion of treatment.

faqs.question: Does my child's birth weight affect their risk of hepatoblastoma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children born with a very low birth weight — under 1,500 grams — have approximately 20 to 40 times the risk of hepatoblastoma compared to normal-birth-weight infants. The risk is thought to relate to disrupted liver development during premature birth. Most VLBW infants are not routinely screened, but parents should report any abdominal mass promptly.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hepatoblastoma and hepatocellular carcinoma in children?
faqs.answer: Hepatoblastoma arises in children under 5 from embryonic liver cells and almost always produces AFP. Hepatocellular carcinoma — more common in older children and adolescents — is associated with underlying liver disease such as hepatitis B or cirrhosis, has a more aggressive biology, and has lower resectability and survival rates than hepatoblastoma.

faqs.question: Should siblings of a child with hepatoblastoma be screened?
faqs.answer: Routine sibling screening is not recommended unless there is a known heritable risk factor such as Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, familial adenomatous polyposis, or an identified germline mutation. When a genetic syndrome is confirmed in the affected child, genetic testing and surveillance for siblings is arranged through clinical genetics.

faqs.question: What support is available for families dealing with hepatoblastoma?
faqs.answer: Specialist paediatric cancer centres offer psychosocial support teams, social workers, child life specialists, and family support programmes. National organisations such as the Children's Oncology Group provide educational resources and connect families with clinical trials. Survivorship programmes address hearing, cardiac, and educational needs long after treatment.

faqs.question: Is hepatoblastoma painful?
faqs.answer: Most hepatoblastomas are initially painless and are discovered as a mass rather than because of pain. As the tumour grows, abdominal discomfort or distension may develop. Sudden severe pain suggests tumour rupture, which is a medical emergency. Chemotherapy-related nausea and discomfort are managed with antiemetics and supportive care.

metaDescription: Hepatoblastoma is the most common liver cancer in children under 5. Learn about AFP markers, PRETEXT staging, chemotherapy and surgery outcomes.
